questions.prompt: Why does Tesla make stationary storage (Powerwall, Megapack) at the same Gigafactories as vehicles?

choices.text: Lack of land for separate factories.

Cells are the common platform. Same cells go into vehicle packs and storage products. Shared engineering investments benefit both. Capex amortizes across more revenue. Demand cycles differ — when EV demand softens, storage demand may not — so plant utilization smooths. Strategic optionality: allocate cells to whichever product has best margin or highest demand at the moment.

questions.explanation: Cell-as-common-platform is the unifier. Shared cells, shared engineering, shared capex amortization, smoothed demand, strategic optionality. The vertical stack is a real operational + strategic advantage, not vanity.

questions.prompt: Lucid makes vehicles only. Northvolt made batteries only. Why hasn't Tesla's stack-everything model been universally copied?

choices.text: Hard to copy — requires multiple product lines built up over years, cell technology has steep learning curves, vertical integration capex is in the billions. Specialized competitors (vehicle-only or battery-only) have simpler operations but lose the cross-product engineering and demand-smoothing dividends. The stack model creates value when integration costs less than benefits — that calculation requires scale and patience few competitors have.

questions.explanation: Stack-everything requires capital, time, and tolerance for organizational complexity. Specialized players (Lucid, Northvolt) optimize differently: simpler operations, narrower bet. Each model has its place; Tesla's bet pays off when integration dividends justify the complexity cost.
